title: How to Create a Weekly Money Date With Yourself
body:
Your Money Date: It's Not What You Think

Truth is, most of you avoid looking at your money. You tiptoe around it, shove statements in drawers, glance nervously at your bank balance, then quickly close the app. You treat your finances like that weird uncle at Thanksgiving - you know he's there, but you really, really don't want to talk to him. And yet, you wonder why you feel so out of control, so anxious, so... broke. You're constantly reacting, never truly creating. You're living in a state of financial fight or flight, and it's exhausting. But here's what's interesting, this avoidance? It's not about the numbers themselves. It's about what those numbers represent to you. It's about shame, fear, inadequacy. It's about deeply ingrained beliefs that tell you you're not good enough, not smart enough, not *worthy* enough to handle money. And until you face that, until you invite that uncomfortable truth into the light, nothing changes. This isn't about budgeting, not really. It's about healing. It's about creating a weekly money date with yourself, not as a chore, but as an act of radical self-care, a conscious choice to repair one of the most fundamental relationships in your life.

Why You're Avoiding Your Money (And Why It's Hurting You)

Let's be blunt. You're avoiding your money because you're afraid of what you'll find. You're afraid of judgment - your own, and some imagined external one. You're afraid of confirming your deepest fears: that you're a failure, that you'll never have enough, that you're somehow fundamentally broken when it comes to finances. Brené Brown's work on shame is incredibly relevant here. Shame thrives in secrecy, silence, and judgment. And when it comes to money, we're swimming in it. We don't talk about our salaries, our debts, our financial struggles. We pretend everything's fine, even when it's not. This secrecy creates a fertile ground for shame to grow, turning financial missteps into personal failings. You feel isolated, like you're the only one who can't get it right. Right?! This avoidance has real consequences. It keeps you stuck. You can't make informed decisions if you don't have accurate information. You can't plan for the future if you don't know where you stand today. You can't build wealth if you're constantly operating from a place of fear and scarcity. This isn't just about missing out on investment opportunities; it's about missing out on peace of mind. It's about living with a low hum of anxiety that infiltrates every other area of your life. Your relationships, your health, your work - they all suffer when your financial life is a mess you refuse to clean up. Lynne Twist, in The Soul of Money (paid link), talks about how our relationship with money mirrors our relationship with life itself. If you're constantly feeling like there's not enough, that you have to hoard, that you're never truly secure, then that's how you're approaching everything. This isn't just about dollars and cents; it's about your core beliefs about abundance, worthiness, and security.

What Is a Weekly Money Date, Really?

Forget everything you think you know about "budgeting" or "financial planning." This isn't about deprivation or spreadsheets filled with tiny numbers that make your eyes glaze over. A weekly money date is a sacred appointment with yourself and your money. It's a conscious, compassionate check-in. It's about bringing awareness, not judgment, to your financial reality. It's about building trust with yourself, and with your money. Think of it as therapy for your finances. You wouldn't go to therapy once a year and expect real change, would you? The same applies to your money. Consistent, gentle attention is key. This isn't a "fix it all" session; it's a dedicated time to observe, to learn, to adjust, and to feel. Yes, to feel. Because money is deeply emotional. When you sit down to look at your bank account, what emotions bubble up? Fear? Anger? Shame? Excitement? Curiosity? Don't push them away. Just notice them. That's the work. That's where the healing begins. This date is about presence. It's about saying, "I see you, money. I see my relationship with you. And I'm willing to show up for it, consistently." It's a commitment. A promise to yourself that you're going to stop running, stop hiding, and start facing your financial reality with courage and curiosity.

Setting the Stage for Your Sacred Money Time

You wouldn't show up for a hot date looking like a mess, would you? Treat your money date with the same respect.

1. Choose Your Time and Place: This needs to be non-negotiable. Put it on your calendar. Block it out. Is it Sunday morning with a cup of coffee? Wednesday evening after the kids are asleep? Find a time when you're not rushed, not stressed, and can truly focus. And choose a quiet, comfortable space where you won't be interrupted. Make it inviting. Light a candle. Put on some soft music. Make it feel special, not like a punishment.

2. Gather Your Tools (The Basics): You don't need fancy software to start. A notebook and pen, access to your online banking, credit card statements, and perhaps a simple spreadsheet. The goal isn't to overcomplicate it; it's to simplify. Barbara Huson's work in "Overcoming Under-earning" emphasizes getting clear on your numbers. You can't overcome anything if you don't know what "anything" is.

3. Set an Intention: Before you even open a statement, take a deep breath. What's your intention for this session? Is it to understand your spending? To track your income? To pay a bill? To simply be present with the numbers without judgment? State it clearly. This grounds you and shifts the energy from dread to purpose.

4. Prime Your Mindset: This is crucial. Before you dive in, remind yourself that you are capable. You are intelligent. You are worthy of financial well-being. This isn't about what you *should* have done. It's about what you *can* do, right now. Alan Watts reminds us that the only way to make sense out of change is to plunge into it, move with it, and join the dance. Your money situation is constantly changing. Don't fight it. Dance with it.

The Anatomy of a Weekly Money Date: What to Actually Do

Okay, you're sitting down. You've got your tea. The candle's lit. Now what?

1. Check-In (5-10 minutes):

  • How do you feel about money this week? Jot down a few words. Don't censor.
  • Review your bank accounts: Just look. What's the balance? Are there any unexpected transactions? Don't judge them, just observe.
  • Review credit card balances: Same thing. Just the numbers.

This is your baseline. It's like taking your financial pulse. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Feel it. Acknowledge it. It's okay. It's just a number.

2. Process Transactions (15-20 minutes):

  • Go through your recent transactions (bank and credit cards). Categorize them. Did you spend on groceries? Entertainment? Bills?
  • This isn't about shame. It's about awareness. "Oh, I spent how much on takeout this week?" Not "I'm so bad for spending on takeout." But "Interesting. I spent X on takeout. How does that make me feel?"
  • This is where you start to see patterns. Are there subscriptions you forgot about? Charges you don't recognize? This is your opportunity to catch them.

3. Pay Bills & Manage Cash Flow (10-15 minutes):

  • What bills are due soon? Pay them. Schedule them. Get them out of the way.
  • Look at your upcoming income. Does it cover your upcoming expenses? If not, what adjustments do you need to make? This is where proactive choices come in.
  • If you're using a simple budget, this is where you'd allocate funds for the upcoming week or month.

4. Vision & Growth (5-10 minutes):

  • This is the most important part, and often the most overlooked. This isn't just about looking backward; it's about looking forward.
  • What's one financial goal you have? Saving for a trip? Paying down debt? Investing?
  • What's one small step you can take this week towards that goal? Even if it's just researching something.
  • Read a chapter from a financial book. I highly recommend The Psychology of Money (paid link) by Morgan Housel. It's short, impactful, and will shift your perspective. Or maybe read a chapter from Jen Sincero's You Are a Badass at Making Money (paid link) for some serious motivation.
  • This isn't just about managing money; it's about creating wealth. And "Wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing."

5. Reflection & Gratitude (2-5 minutes):

  • What did you learn today? What surprised you?
  • What are you grateful for financially? Even if it's just that you have a roof over your head, or that you could afford your coffee this morning. Cultivating gratitude shifts your scarcity mindset.
  • Thank yourself for showing up. For being brave. For taking this time.

This entire process shouldn't take more than an hour. And it might feel clunky and awkward at first. You feel that? That's normal. Keep showing up.

The Deeper Work: Beyond the Numbers

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? Think about it. Your money doesn't lie. It shows you exactly where your priorities are, where your fears are, where your values truly lie. It reflects your habits, your beliefs, your self-worth. When you consistently show up for your money date, you're not just tracking expenses. You're developing self-awareness. You're confronting your financial shadow. You're learning to trust yourself. You're building a muscle of financial discipline, not through harsh restriction, but through conscious choice. This isn't about being perfect. You'll make mistakes. You'll overspend sometimes. You'll miss a bill. It's okay. The point isn't flawless execution; it's consistent engagement. It's about creating a safe space for your financial self to exist, without judgment. This practice is a radical act of self-love. It's saying, "My financial well-being matters. And I matter enough to give it my attention." Know what I mean? This isn't just about having more money. It's about having more freedom, more peace, more alignment with your true self. It's about dismantling the societal conditioning that tells you money is evil, or that you're not good enough to have it. It's about reclaiming your power.

Troubleshooting Your Money Date

"I feel overwhelmed." Start small. Seriously. For the first few weeks, just log in to your bank account and look at the balance. That's it. Gradually add one small task each week. Five minutes is better than zero minutes.

"I don't have time." You have time for what you prioritize. This is about prioritizing your mental and financial well-being. Can you swap 30 minutes of scrolling social media for 30 minutes of money awareness?

"I'm afraid of what I'll find." This is precisely why you need to do it. The fear of the unknown is often worse than the reality. Once you know, you can make a plan. You gain agency. You gain control. You might find things are better than you thought. Or you might find things are worse, but now you have the data to change them. Ignorance isn't bliss when it comes to your finances; it's bondage.

"It's boring." Make it less boring! Play your favorite music. Treat yourself to a fancy coffee afterward. Frame it as detective work, unraveling the mystery of your money. The more you connect it to your dreams and goals, the less boring it becomes. It becomes purpose-driven. Does that land?

"I tried it once and it didn't stick." So what? You fell off the wagon. Get back on. This isn't a pass/fail test. It's a practice. Like meditation, like exercise, you'll have good days and bad days. The key is to keep coming back. Forgive yourself, learn, and try again.

Your money date isn't just about managing money. It's about managing your relationship with yourself, your relationship with fear, your relationship with possibility. It's about transforming scarcity into sufficiency, and fear into freedom. It’s about understanding that money is a tool, an energy, a reflection. And when you treat it with respect, when you truly see it, it begins to serve you in ways you never thought possible.

FAQs About Your Weekly Money Date

Q: How long should my weekly money date be?

A: Start with 30-45 minutes. As you get more comfortable, you can extend it to an hour. The key is consistency, not duration. Even 15 minutes of focused attention is incredibly powerful if done every week.

Q: What if I miss a week (or several)?

A: Don't beat yourself up. Life happens. Just pick it up the next week. The goal isn't perfection, it's persistence. Forgive yourself and recommit. The important thing is to not let a missed week turn into a missed month or year.

Q: Do I need special software or apps?

A: Not at all, especially when you're starting. Online banking, a spreadsheet (like Google Sheets), and a simple notebook and pen are perfectly sufficient. The focus is on awareness and action, not on complex tools. As you become more comfortable, you might explore apps if they genuinely simplify your process, but don't let tool selection be an excuse for inaction.

Q: Can I do this with my partner?

A: Absolutely! If you have a partner, a joint money date can be incredibly beneficial for financial alignment and transparency. However, I still recommend you maintain your individual money date first. You need to get clear on your own relationship with money before you bring someone else into that space. Once you're comfortable, you can schedule a separate "couple's money date" to review joint finances and goals.

So, what's one tiny, almost ridiculously small step you can take today to honor your money? Just one. Maybe it's opening your bank app and looking at your balance for 60 seconds. Maybe it's picking a time for your first money date. Do it now. Don't wait until you "feel ready." You'll never feel ready. Just start.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Take control of your finances. Learn how to set up a weekly "money date" with yourself to track spending, set goals, and build financial confidence.
author: Kalesh
